I found these to be the biggest path to help explain why some things work for people and trigger ch in other folks. It's not concrete but it is directly derived from my own labs I had before and during my ch cycle. I would be happy to explain any of it to the community so if you have any questions please ask. I hope this can help us gain some needed traction against the beast.
# SIX-LOOP DIAGNOSTIC FRAMEWORK FOR CLUSTER HEADACHE
**A Personalized, Biomarker-Based Approach to Treatment**
Daniel Huddleston • 2026
---
## THE PROBLEM & THE SOLUTION: THE HOUSE FIRE ANALOGY
A cluster headache is up to six "fires" (loops) burning at once. Treating blind is like using the wrong extinguisher.
## THE PROTOCOL: TWO-DRAW BLOOD TEST
1. **BASELINE DRAW** (No Pain)
2. **CYCLE DRAW** (Active Pain)
3. **COMPARE & FIND THE "DELTA"**
4. **TREAT THE ACTIVE LOOPS**
Simple blood tests, ordered together, reveal which loops are running too hot.
---
## THE SIX LOOPS
### Loop 1: The Chain Reaction
**What it is & feels like:**
Nitric Oxide (NO) release triggers rapid spread. Feels like rapid-onset pain, escalates in seconds/minutes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
NO Metabolites (Nitrate/Nitrite)
**Targeted Treatments:**
High-flow oxygen, Triptans
---
### Loop 2: The Survival Shield
**What it is & feels like:**
Cell survival pathway activated, cells protect themselves. Feels like attacks return at same intensity daily.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum Tryptase
**Targeted Treatments:**
Prednisone (first round only)
---
### Loop 3: The Pain Relay
**What it is & feels like:**
CGRP protein carries pain signal, dilates vessels. Feels like intense pressure behind one eye, attacks chain together.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum CGRP
**Targeted Treatments:**
Emgality (galcanezumab)
---
### Loop 4: The Sensitivity Dial
**What it is & feels like:**
Histamine release sensitizes nerves and vessels. Feels like sinus congestion, fullness, allergy-like.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Plasma Histamine, Urine N-methylhistamine
**Targeted Treatments:**
Antihistamines, Mast cell stabilizers (e.g., ketotifen)
---
### Loop 5: The Stress Engine
**What it is & feels like:**
Hypothalamus releases stress hormone CRH. Feels like clockwork attacks, worse with stress, blood sugar spikes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Cortisol, Fasting Glucose
**Targeted Treatments:**
Verapamil, Vitamin D3 regimen
---
### Loop 6: The Slow Burn
**What it is & feels like:**
Recruitment of inflammatory cells re-triggers mast cells. Feels like a bout that won't end, feeling "inflamed."
**Blood Test Markers:**
CRP, ESR, IL-6, TNF-alpha
**Targeted Treatments:**
Corticosteroids, Anti-inflammatory protocols
---
## WHY IT MATTERS
It is not "treatment resistance." The variation is measurable. The map connects tests to existing treatments.
---

Hey Eagleswings,
You've received some excellent advise loading and upping your vitamin D3 maintenance dose to 10,000 IU/day.  Make sure you're taking all the supplements in the basic anti-inflammatory regimen illustrated in the photo below of what I take and suggest to CHers and Migraineurs.
My preference is to use the Bio-Tech D3-50 50,000 IU water soluble vitamin D3 as a loading dose.
With your last 25(OH)D3 assay at 86 ng/mL, you're at the low end of the average CHer optimum response range (80 to 100 ng/mL).
As we gain roughly 10 ng/mL in serum 25(OH)D3 concentration for every 100,000 IU of oral vitamin D3 taken, I'd take 50,000 IU/day for five days then drop back to either 10,000 IU/day with the oil-based vitamin D3 liquid softgels or 50,000 IU/week taking one of the Bio-Tech D3-50 capsules as your maintenance dose.  The Bio-Tech D3-50 has a higher bioequivalence than the same dose of the vitamin D3 liquid softgels so you can get by with slightly less taking the D3-50.  The Bio-Tech D3-50 is also the least expensive form of vitamin D3 at 23 cents USD per capsule.
I'd also start the Antihistamine Full Monty.  As you didn't experience a CH pain free response with your 25(OH)D3 serum concentration at 86 ng/mL, it's a safe bet you're battling an immune system response to allergens.  When insulted by allergens, the immune system's Mast Cells degranulate (spit open and dump their contents) releasing large quantities of inflammatory mediators as illustrated in the following graphic.
Leading this pack of inflammatory mediators is Histamine.  Histamine to a CHer is like Kryptonite to Superman - Bad News as none of the standards of care preventatives like verapamil, MM or vitamin D3 will work.  That means you need to treat the immune system response to allergens.  This is where the Antihistamine Full Monty comes into play.
The Antihistamine Full Monty is a collection of supplements with antihistamine properties that include 3 grams/day each of Turmeric (Curcumin), Resveratrol, Quercetin and Omega-3 fatty acids.  It also includes 200 mcg/day Selenium, 8 grams/day vitamin C and 10 mg/day Melatonin taken at bed time.
I buy the bulk powdered vitamin C in 1 Kg bags and stir 2 level teaspoon measures in 8 ounces of water and take sips all day until it's gone by bedtime.  This is the least expensive form of vitamin C and dosing throughout the day is best as our kidneys filter vitamin C rapidly.
I realize this is a boatload of pills to take, but the Full Monty is proving to be very effective in achieving a CH pain free response.  Most CHers respond to the Antihistamine Full Monty during the first week.  When you've experienced a sustained CH pain free response for at least a week, you can lower the dose of the first four supplements to 2 grams/day.  I'd stay at that dose for at least two weeks then taper the dose down to 1 gram a day.  You can skip the selenium and melatonin at that point, but I'd still take at least 4 grams/day vitamin C as it's so important

Hi Scott! Welcome to the Board. Glad that you found us!
Well, you pharmacist may know meds, but not CH. The front line med would be Verapamil, starting at about 240mg/day and working up. A Prednisone taper is usually given at the beginning for a week or so to get you some pain free time while the Verap builds up in your body Long term use of Prednisone is not good for your body and should be avoided. The Verap dose can go up to over 900mg/day for some. It needs to be monitored with dose increases. And Imitrex is given in shot form for the fastest abort for CH. Nasal beats the pills given for migraines, but is not as fast as the injection. Pills work too slow for CH and should only be given for migraines. As you have found out, you cannot get enough of it to treat all of your hits. And you can't take an injection or spray for every hit! Most will load up on Trex when out of cycle to be better prepared when they get one. It is hard on the heart and the daily use is limited to two in 24 hours. Insurance usually only approves 9 to 12 a month. Which does not begin to treat them all!!! There is a way to split the injections into 3 doses that helps a lot of people. The full dose is usually more than is needed, especially when gotten to quickly. That does up your number of hits that you can kill with it. However, Trex is a double-edged sword - it can cause rebound hits and you wind up with an escalating cycle of more and more hits.
Ch has a lovely ability to morph over time. Frequency of hits changes, dates change, etc. Sorry that you have had this happen. Nocturnal hits sort of suck big time. Often by the time you wake, it is way up there on the pain scale. You can try sleeping in a recliner with it partly reclined - keep your heart above your head. It may not prevent your hits, but you will wake sooner to deal with one. That can be a huge help!!
Another help is Energy Drinks or Shots. Caffeine helps a lot! Some feel the Taurine in the drinks makes them work better than coffee, but for those who can't take them, coffee is great too. I keep it cold in the fridge for the nights. Grab it on the way to the O2 tank and get a faster abort. By itself, with a bit of deep inhales and hard exhales of room air can sometimes kill a hit too.
Pure O2 at 15 to 25 LPM will be your best friend for aborts. Not a concentrator! You will use a non-rebreather mask, so no room air is inhaled. Basically you force out the CO2 with a hard exhale with a crunch at the end and then a big inhale, hold for a bit, and repeat. You dial your O2 up to support the breathing - don't want to wait for the bag on the mask to fill, you need it waiting for that inhale. When the pain is gone, then you do your 'post hit' breathing. Dial it down and breath normally for five minutes or so. This prevents an immediate return of the hit. If your insurance covers it, get it! If not, then we can get you set up with welding O2 to use. No doctor needed then.
